words: SAM SCHRAGER: What was it your parents heard about this mill coming in here and dec
ided to come over here themselves?
ARTHUR SUNDBERG: Well, where we lived in Wisconsin— we lived way up in the north
east corner of Wisconsin— just a few miles from Michigan, and of course,
that was lumbering area, and the big economy in that whole part of the
country was lumbering, you know, years ago. But it began to peter out, be
cause, you know, well, when they logged, they logged. They just cut every
thing, and of course the stuff didn't grow back as fast. It isn't like what
they do now-a-days, this selective logging.

speaker: SS
words: They were just cutting out? Just cutting out and not replanting?

speaker: AS
words: That's right. And then, of course, the bigger outfitSj they began to move

speaker: AS
words: West because, oh, years before, why, as the railroads moved out— the rail

speaker: AS
words: roads were granted every other section on both sides of the track all the way

speaker: AS
words: across the country, and the railroads had an awful lot of timber land, you

speaker: AS
words: know. And, of course, that was a big inducement for some of these lumbering

speaker: AS
words: people too to start moving out this way, and they come out and invested in

speaker: AS
words: timber land and they got it for,just imagine— practically for a song- be

speaker: AS
words: cause, you know values were way low those days. So they accumulated quite a

speaker: AS
words: scope of timber. Well, then, of course, when they started to build this plant

speaker: AS
words: there was several men that worked in this lumber mill in the town where we

speaker: AS
words: come from, where my dad worked, they moved out here and worked on construct

speaker: AS
words: ion work and then they went back and they was telling others about the big

speaker: AS
words: money out here, you know. Well, at the time we moved out here in the spring

speaker: AS
words: of 1909— back in that area where we lived in Wisconsin, men that piled green

speaker: AS
words: lumber in a lumberyard for ten hours a day got a dollar and sixty-five cents

speaker: AS
words: for ten hours work piling green lumber. And then, of course, the word come

speaker: AS
words: back here that out here they were paying two dollars and a quarter an hour

speaker: AS
words: for common labor. Just any common labor, you know, two dollars and a quarter—

speaker: AS
words: a day, I meant, not an hour. That is for a ten hour day. Well, that was quite

speaker: AS
words: a thing. My dad he was a lumber grader back there and he got, as I say,lum

speaker: AS
words: ber pilers got a dollar and sixty-five cents a day— my dad got two dollars

speaker: AS
words: and a quarter a day for grading lumber, ten hours. But then when he ctome

speaker: AS
words: out he got three dollars and a half a day; That was quite a boost, you see

speaker: AS
words: And that's what brought people out. There wasn't any lumber people that

speaker: AS
words: were native to this part of the country, you know. They all had to come

speaker: AS
words: from back in the Midwest or in the East, you see. And, of course, the Mid

speaker: AS
words: west- oh, Michigan, Wisconsin and Minnesota was the three principal logging

speaker: AS
words: areas of the United States at that time. 'Course they had lots of it down

speaker: AS
words: south. It really wasn't recognized as a logging area, that Southern pine.

speaker: AS
words: So that's how it come that people migrated out this way. Well, of course,

speaker: AS
words: when they come out here thewas- they built up- they had five sawmills

speaker: AS
words: in Coeur d'Alene. And then there was sawmills in Harrison on the Lake and

speaker: AS
words: at Springston, and then, of course, up north of Coeur d'Alene there was saw

speaker: AS
words: mills at Sandpoint and Bonners Ferry and Laclede and sawmills all over the

speaker: AS
words: country, you see. And all these people that- operating these sawmillsthey

speaker: AS
words: migrated out here from the Midwest.

speaker: SS
words: Did they all start out at the turn of the century?

speaker: AS
words: Yeah, uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: Did your family have very deep roots in the Midwest logging?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, no. Well, my parents they immigrated here from Sweden in along about

speaker: AS
words: 1890, you see, and, of course, my dad had worked at that kind of work over

speaker: AS
words: in Sweden, because that's quite a logging area over there, you know. And

speaker: AS
words: so, of course, when they come over herejthey just naturally moved into the

speaker: AS
words: logging and lumbering work, you see.

speaker: SS
words: I just can't help but to take you back a little farther here and ask you, you

speaker: SS
words: knew why they decided to leave Sweden and come to America.

speaker: AS
words: Well, it was strictly economic, because— well, at that time, you know the

speaker: AS
words: boatloads of immigrants— they were unloading them at the docks in New York.

speaker: AS
words: Just as fast as they could unload one ship they'd move another one in and

speaker: AS
words: unload that. That's as fast as the immigrants were coming into this country

speaker: AS
words: from Europe} particular see. Because— now, of course, I wasn't there

speaker: AS
words: but then I know the story and of course all these immigrants they come over

speaker: AS
words: and they'd have a big tag fastened to their jacket, 'cause none of 'em could

speaker: AS
words: speak any English, there'd be destination printed on there, you know, so

speaker: AS
words: these officials could shunt 'em this way or that way. These labor contract

speaker: AS
words: ors they would meet these boats in New York and they would try to induce these

speaker: AS
words: people that was coming off'n the boat to hire out to them, see. And, of course

speaker: AS
words: they got paid so much a head for signing 'em up, you know. And you can imag

speaker: AS
words: ine all the miles and miles of ditches that had to be dug in New York and

speaker: AS
words: Philadelphia and Boston and all the cities, you know. It was all done by

speaker: AS
words: hand labor, you know, for all the water pipes and all thesewer systems and

speaker: AS
words: that was all done by hand labor so it took a tremendous amount of people.

speaker: AS
words: And,.they tried to induce these people to sign up for them because every time

speaker: AS
words: they did they'd make fifty cents a head, or whatever it was they paid at that

speaker: AS
words: time. And they made big money on them. But then there was these other people

speaker: AS
words: that— like telling about my folks come from— 'course my folks were married

speaker: AS
words: in this country, they didn't know each other there. But they had a destin

speaker: AS
words: ation in mind when they left, because they knew exactly where they were going.

speaker: AS
words: And they was heading right straight for Menominee, Michigan.

speaker: SS
words: Relatives? Friends?

speaker: AS
words: No, it was friends, you know they'd corresponded and they had friends there.

speaker: AS
words: And, well, my mother, she had two brothers that settled in that part of the

speaker: AS
words: country, and oh, they were around the Chicago area. But they didn't work in

speaker: AS
words: the logging and lumbering industry, they were both carpenters and builders.

speaker: AS
words: They were just construction people. But then that's the reason they had a

speaker: AS
words: destination in mind when they come over, and then, of course, after they get

speaker: AS
words: here it's just a matter of just kinda joining in with the rest of 'em. And,

speaker: AS
words: of course,— it's a funny thing about the people that emigrated over in—

speaker: AS
words: back the turn of the century. Now the town that we come from— Dunbar,

speaker: AS
words: Wisconsin, and it was just a small town, had one lumber mill. But all the

speaker: AS
words: Swedes lived in a little group in one corner of this little town. And all

speaker: AS
words: the French Canadians, they lived in this other little group; and all the Finns

speaker: AS
words: from Finland, they lived in another group; and all the foles, they lived in

speaker: AS
words: this other group. And, you know, it's a funny thing but you take the — oh

speaker: AS
words: the women in particular, they could live here for— well, I know some of

speaker: AS
words: 'em lived here fifty years or more and never did learn to speak English.

speaker: AS
words: Because they lived in this little community where they spoke their native

speaker: AS
words: tongue, see. Of course the men they had to go out and work, they had to

speaker: AS
words: learn some English, you know. But that's just the way the people were that

speaker: AS
words: immigrated over here.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of relations were there between or among the different groups?

speaker: SS
words: Hostility?

speaker: AS
words: It was. Relations wasn't too good. I know from what I'd seen myself. We

speaker: AS
words: had a few Irish and the Irish were kind of cocky, overbearing sort of men,

speaker: AS
words: you know. And they always wanted to flex their muscles. And, of course,

speaker: AS
words: there was ankwful lot of drinking of alcoholic beverages them days. And

speaker: AS
words: they'd always, want to pick a fight. But in that little community where we

speaker: AS
words: lived, they generally came out on the short end. . If they tangled with a

speaker: AS
words: Finn, they'd get cut from one end to the other because a Finn was never

speaker: AS
words: known to ever— I think he even slept with his knives— they carried knives

speaker: AS
words: all the time, you know. And they used 'em, too. And I don't think that you—

speaker: AS
words: Very seldom that you couldsee a Finn but what he had scars all over himself

speaker: AS
words: you know, where they'd fight among themselves. And, so, they always come out

speaker: AS
words: on the short end if they tangled with them, you know, in a drunken brawl.

speaker: AS
words: And we had lots of 'em. .Lots of drunken brawls, every weekend, you know,

speaker: AS
words: you could see 'em out in the street there just really going at it.

speaker: SS
words: What would usually start fights like that? Liquor?

speaker: AS
words: Yeah. It's the same thing today. You take people that go into these tav

speaker: AS
words: erns and bars, some of 'em that can't hold it, you know, they get quarrel

speaker: AS
words: some and then they want to pick a fight, and maybe some big guy that's trying

speaker: AS
words: to show how big he is, and then some little guy'11 crack him and really lay

speaker: AS
words: it to him. You know, that's the way it works out. Some little guy'11 gen

speaker: AS
words: erally just really work that big fellow over.

speaker: AS
words: Well, yeah. There's lots of little funny things that goes on. I can re

speaker: AS
words: call one Sunday back there in Wisconsin. They had— it was quite swampy

speaker: AS
words: around there—there was quite a few of them big bull snakes around there,

speaker: AS
words: you know, and I don't know— there was one kind there that people there called

speaker: AS
words: 'em a pine snake— but they were just a big bull snake, had big spots all

speaker: AS
words: over 'em, you know. But, anyway this— there was a Frenchman there and he

speaker: AS
words: had a— raising quite a few hogs, and he had some land that was in this

speaker: AS
words: swampy area andKhad his hogs around there, and so he killed one of them darn

speaker: AS
words: big bull snakesone Saturday. And these Finns they got out there Sunday

speaker: AS
words: morning in the street and they were fighting among themselves, and they were

speaker: AS
words: really laying it to 'em, you know. So this Frenchmanhe thought he'd break

speaker: AS
words: up this brawl, you know, so he had that big bull snake by the tail and he come

speaker: AS
words: a dragging it behind him, and he got down there right in the middle of that

speaker: AS
words: bunch and he raised it up and shook it in the air, and it looked like it was

speaker: AS
words: alive. You know, they took after him and he throwed his snake down and he

speaker: AS
words: run for home as hard as he could go and he just barely got in the house and

speaker: AS
words: he grabbed a rifle and stood 'em off, because they was gonna kill him,sure.

speaker: AS
words: And that's one way to start one.

speaker: SS
words: Were there any guys back there that were known for being really tough men?

speaker: SS
words: That had a reputation.

speaker: AS
words: Well, they had a reputation locally, butftit didn't go out very far. Probably

speaker: AS
words: people in the next town didn't know about it, you know. But in that little

speaker: AS
words: area there,there was a few that were known to be sort of trouble makers and

speaker: AS
words: so on, and, of course, most of the people kind of shied away from 'em, you

speaker: AS
words: know, to just keep from getting into trouble. But then that's just the life

speaker: AS
words: of the lumbering industry,"part of the lumbering industry, part of it s growing

speaker: AS
words: up. And, these loggers, the ones that worked in the woods, they all

speaker: AS
words: wore corked (caulked) boots and, you know, they'd never take 'em off; they'd

speaker: AS
words: probably take 'em off when they went to bed and I doubt that too sometimes.

speaker: AS
words: But, anyway, if they come in the house or went anywhere Sunday or any other

speaker: AS
words: day they had them corked boots, Kthat was an emblem of their trade. Any

speaker: AS
words: time that you seen a man with corked boots you knew he was a logger, see.

speaker: AS
words: And, of course, they werejroud of that distinction, too. They was supposed

speaker: AS
words: to be tough. But then, this little town where we lived they wouldn't allow

speaker: AS
words: any saloons in that town, butW was just ten miles from there, and

speaker: AS
words: was right on the Sioux Line railroad and the passenger train used to run—

speaker: AS
words: it was just right so that after work in the evening when they got through

speaker: AS
words: work, they could get on the train and ride up to and get off and

speaker: AS
words: drink until midnight and then catch a train back again. Well, by the time

speaker: AS
words: they was ready to come back, or possiblythey'd stay overnight and come back

speaker: AS
words: on Sunday, why— on this railroad platform thfgy'd be pretty well gowed up

speaker: AS
words: with booze. And, of course, most of 'em'd have a suitcase with 'em full of

speaker: AS
words: booze, but an awful lot of the times they'd get into a big brawl right on

speaker: AS
words: the platform there and they'd really go at 'er you know.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard that when a guy was down that he could figure on being spiked

speaker: SS
words: with the corked boots.

speaker: AS
words: Oh, that's one reason they wore 'em all the time. There was nothing fair

speaker: AS
words: about fighting. If they could knock 'em down, then they'd go at em with

speaker: AS
words: their boots and they'd just cut 'em from one end to the other, they didn't

speaker: AS
words: care whether it was his face arms legs or any place. The blood'd

speaker: AS
words: be running in every direction. They were tough.

speaker: SS
words: You know, I've heard, along this line, that after a fight like that, that

speaker: SS
words: the guys who were fighting wouldn't really hold it against each other, once

speaker: SS
words: they sobered up. It was something that was forgotten.

speaker: AS
words: Chances are when they sobered up the next day, why, they couldn't remember

speaker: AS
words: they'd been in a fight! They was all cut up, "Where'd I get this?" But

speaker: AS
words: you know as far as the people migrating out this way from the lumber industry

speaker: AS
words: back there, there was— the big majority of the people that come out here to

speaker: AS
words: work in the lumber industry were Scandivanian descent. There was a few Poles

speaker: AS
words: that come out here but they didn't stay long. Most of 'em just out here

speaker: AS
words: a short while and.they went back again. And, I don't know of any of the

speaker: AS
words: FrenchVanadian that ever come out here. They all stayed back there and—

speaker: AS
words: oh, whenever they would leave our area back there they would probably go

speaker: AS
words: back up into Quebec or up in that country, see. But those that come out here

speaker: AS
words: were mostly Scandivanians.

speaker: SS
words: I've got one more question. You were just saying about the saloon being ten

speaker: SS
words: miles away— even though it was ten miles away, there was still brawling in

speaker: SS
words: the town, itself?

speaker: AS
words: No. In this town where the saloons were, you see, they'd be on the depot

speaker: AS
words: platform waiting to catch the train to go home, and while they was waiting

speaker: AS
words: 'course when they were sober they got along pretty good.

speaker: SS
words: There wasn't actually too much going on in the little town where you grew

speaker: SS
words: up?

speaker: AS
words: There was lots of booze there, but they didn't have any saloons, see. Now

speaker: AS
words: then they shipped in beer on the railroad, in fact of matter, there was just

speaker: AS
words: about a half a mile from our house, there was a spot in the woods there, that

speaker: AS
words: they called a beer garden. And, you know they had some beautiful trees back

speaker: AS
words: in that country. There was oak and maple and ash and hickory and everything

speaker: AS
words: you can think of, we had back there, you know, trees with leaves on 'em, and

speaker: AS
words: there was an area there these men had laid some logs around where they

speaker: AS
words: could sit around, quite an open space in between, and it was probably fortyfifty

speaker: AS
words: yards in diameter, in this circle, and there was all trees around there,

speaker: AS
words: and that's what everybody in the area around there designated as a beer gard

speaker: AS
words: en. Well, every Monday morning us kids'd go up there and we'd gather up the

speaker: AS
words: beer bottles and we'd take 'em down to the depot, you know, and I think we

speaker: AS
words: got ten cents a case for the bottles, and of course that's a big thing for us

speaker: AS
words: kids, because it was about the only way we could get any money. And them

speaker: AS
words: beer kegs, you know, we'd go up there and roll them empty beer kegs down. We

speaker: AS
words: got a dime for each one of them. And there was lots of 'em up there every

speaker: AS
words: Monday morning, because they'd ship it in and they'd start in as soon as they

speaker: AS
words: got through work Saturday night. And even men with families, they wouldn't

speaker: AS
words: even think about going home, they just stayed right there and soaked it up

speaker: AS
words: until Monday morning, and then they'd shake their head and go back to workI

speaker: SS
words: Wonder how the wives accepted that kind of thing in those days.

speaker: AS
words: Well, I the majority of 'em didn't know any different. That was just

speaker: AS
words: part of life, that was all. It was up to the women to raise the kids and see

speaker: AS
words: that they got something to eat. The man, he could go and spend his money on

speaker: AS
words: booze. It was a hard life.

speaker: SS
words: Was it a company town? Was that why there were no saloons?

speaker: AS
words: No, well, of course the company owned — had the sawmill and they owned the

speaker: AS
words: store, but then everybody owned their own home. But I don't know, for some

speaker: AS
words: reason never get any saloons started there.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like they really didn't need 'em.

speaker: AS
words: Oh, no, no. And this town was the same way. When they started this town

speaker: AS
words: the company owned everything; all the buildings, houses and everything. But

speaker: AS
words: that's one thing that was laid down right from the beginning, there'd be no

speaker: AS
words: saloons. Now down here at Palouse, ten miles away, there was ten or twelve

speaker: AS
words: saloons there, up here at Princeton, two-three miles away, there was three

speaker: AS
words: saloons up there. They didn't need it, you know, they had lots of saloons

speaker: AS
words: around.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a Weyerhaeuser operation?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, no. That was a Wells Lumber Company.

speaker: SS
words: Was there a lot of broken families in that kind of a situation?

speaker: AS
words: No. I never heard tell of a divorce until— gosh, I don't know, I guess I

speaker: AS
words: was grown up before I ever heard tell of anybody getting a divorce, fgc

speaker: AS
words: they just accepted that as a way of life, that's all.

speaker: SS
words: Well most of the people in that town, were they millers or loggers?

speaker: AS
words: It was all logging and lumbering. You see, the loggers was out in the woods

speaker: AS
words: and, of course, they had their families in town, and then, of course, all

speaker: AS
words: these people at the sawmill, of course, they all lived there, and so that

speaker: AS
words: was the main industry. There was a few for the railroad. There was two

speaker: AS
words: railroads that went through there. And, of course, that took some people

speaker: AS
words: to maintain these railroads, you know. Principally, it was lumbering.

speaker: SS
words: It sounds as though, to me, that as a kid growing up there, you must have

speaker: SS
words: really got to see a lot of what life was all about.

speaker: AS
words: Well, that's true. I think possibly you might get the same reaction from

speaker: AS
words: any man of my age or older, that kids matured an awful lot quicker than they

speaker: AS
words: do now. You take— oh, I think I told you when you were here the other day

speaker: AS
words: that they'd hire kids twelve years old to work around the mill. And they

speaker: AS
words: chewed tobacco and smoked and they'd drink when they could get ahold of it.

speaker: AS
words: They couldn't buy it but then they'd get ahold of it. And you take when the

speaker: AS
words: average boy was fifteen or sixteen he was just as much matured as a fellow

speaker: AS
words: is right now at thirty. Just the way they lived. I knew lots of

speaker: AS
words: boys in the fourth grade in school that shewed tobacco just like any guy you

speaker: AS
words: ever seen.

speaker: SS
words: What did you think of the difference in that kind of lumber town and what

speaker: SS
words: you found out here?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, well, this here was just like coming into heaven, coming out here. There

speaker: AS
words: was one thing that was really impressive to us at that time. We left back

speaker: AS
words: there on the 20th day of April and the snow was still clear up to the eaves

speaker: AS
words: on the small houses, and they were still driving their sleighs right across,

speaker: AS
words: right over the top of fences and everything, and that was the 20th of

speaker: AS
words: April. 'Course that was a tough winter that year. But when we come out

speaker: AS
words: here, it took us four days to get here, why, the kids were all running barefoot

speaker: AS
words: and stuff was green and nice and warm. Andthat's quite a transition

speaker: AS
words: to go from, in four days time, from what we had there to what they had here.

speaker: AS
words: And, then, of course everything was booming here. This mill had beenrunning

speaker: AS
words: for about two and a half years, I guess, a little better, and they never said

speaker: AS
words: "No." to anybody that had guts enough to ask for a job. They hired— that

speaker: AS
words: is men— we never heard tell of 'em hiring women— but men or boys, if they

speaker: AS
words: asked for a job, if they looked like they could work, why, they'd put 'em

speaker: AS
words: on. And they'd fire 'em just as easy as they hired 'em. There was no unions

speaker: AS
words: there was nothing; the boss he wasking-pin, and what he said went. He d

speaker: AS
words: hire you and he'd fire you, and he could fire you for the very least little

speaker: AS
words: thing, too, sometimes. But if he didn't like you, why, that was it, you've

speaker: AS
words: had it!

speaker: SS
words: Was the town itself more attractive? Was there more to it? Did it seem like

speaker: SS
words: a better place to live?

speaker: AS
words: Yeah. Yeah. It was pretty well laid out when they built this little town. In

speaker: AS
words: fact, they had a board fence around every lot in town. Every lot in town had

speaker: AS
words: a— oh, there was about three one-by-sixes high, horizontally, with a space

speaker: AS
words: in between and then a catboard on top."SoIt was really attractive fence, and

speaker: AS
words: every lot had a front gate and a back gate. And, of course, that was the way

speaker: AS
words: people lived those days, all over the country. You could go anywhere in the

speaker: AS
words: United States and you could find, everybody had a fence around their place.

speaker: AS
words: And everything was new and painted and the company had set out two trees in

speaker: AS
words: front of every house, out in the street. They were all small,possibly the

speaker: AS
words: average tree must have been eight or ten feet tall when they planted them.

speaker: AS
words: And they were maple trees and elm trees and you know, different kinds like

speaker: AS
words: that. They didn't plant any of the native trees, it was all trees that had

speaker: AS
words: leaves on them. And, of course, the native trees here are all pine and firs

speaker: AS
words: and stuff like that. They really laid it out to make a nice little town out

speaker: AS
words: of it. And, oh, it stayed that way up until about the time of World War I,

speaker: AS
words: and just shortly after that, then, of course, first one and then another

speaker: AS
words: one would tear out this or tear out that, or something. And then of course,

speaker: AS
words: they had wood sidewalks all over every street was a wood sidewalk on both

speaker: AS
words: sides of the street and up and down town and every which way. They made lots

speaker: AS
words: of lumber and they used lots of lumber, too.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have an easy time finding a place to stay starting in here?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, yeah. Right away we— we stayed with friends for, oh, I don't know, I

speaker: AS
words: think about three four days. But then there was a vacant house that we

speaker: AS
words: got right away. But then, of course, we didn't ship our furniture out from

speaker: AS
words: the East, so we had to buy new furniture,. So it was I think about four ,

speaker: AS
words: days that we stayed with friends and then we moved into our own house. And

speaker: AS
words: then, of course, they had an awful lot of single people, or men that didn't

speaker: AS
words: have their families here. And they had a hotel, a pretty good sized hotel,

speaker: AS
words: and then this hotel had an annex building that was just as big as the hotel

speaker: AS

words: the

speaker: SS
words: You know, Axel Anderson told me how he decided to come to work with Potlatch,

speaker: SS
words: was he was looking at the boards in Spokane to see what was avail

speaker: SS
words: able for work— this was 1907— and he said, he'd already been on a few

speaker: SS
words: jobs, around a lot of jobs, in fact, where he'd worked and the wages had

speaker: SS
words: been pretty good and he just didn't like it. Conditions were bad, one

speaker: SS
words: reason or another he just didn't like it. So this time he decided he'd

speaker: SS
words: find a place that offered the least wages on the board, go there and

speaker: SS
words: try that and see if it was any better and he said that was Potlatch,

speaker: SS
words: at the time compared to these others. But I guess he decided that what

speaker: SS
words: they give you in salary alone didn't maybe mean all that much, might

words: be other things about the people you're working for that would make it

speaker: SS

speaker: AS
words: Well, of course, you take as far as Axel Anderson is concerned.he

speaker: AS
words: worked in the woods all his life and if you was ever in a lumber camp

speaker: AS
words: and you'd see how some of those people eat, and see the food that is

speaker: AS
words: prepared for 'em, you could see why they wanted to make that their way

speaker: AS
words: of life, because, boy, they really did eat!! Now you take back in the

speaker: AS
words: days before refrigeration, why it was pretty hard to keep meat, especial

speaker: AS
words: ly, but they were pretty ingenous in some ways. Now I seen— I've

speaker: AS
words: been in some of those Potlatch logging camps and they didn't have any

speaker: AS
words: refrigeration, but their meathouse was constructed of canvas, you know,

speaker: AS
words: it had a wood floor and low wood sidewalls in it but then it was just

speaker: AS
words: a big tent deal, but they kept a stream of water running over this on

speaker: AS
words: the outside all the time, day and night, that took care of all the evaporation

speaker: AS
words: and it was a cooler in itself, you see. And they could keep

speaker: AS
words: meat fresh in there for several days. And, of course, their cured meats

speaker: AS
words: like hams and bacons and stuff, why that would keep for quite a little

speaker: AS
words: while. And their eggs and all that stuff they kept in there, and of

speaker: AS
words: course, their supply was replenished you know just every few days. But

speaker: AS
words: they didn't have the contamination androtten conditions like they had

speaker: AS
words: in some camps, you know-They were in pretty good shape.

speaker: AS

speaker: AS

speaker: AS
words: work in the logging camp he carried his own bedroll on his back. And,
